# Transcrição: 01-WEBINAR

- **Fonte:** 01-WEBINAR
- **Duração:** 1:04:17
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

If given the choice, I would tell you a five minute story every single time. I love a five minute story. I think five minutes is almost always the perfect length of a story. It's where I got my training to begin with. I started telling stories for the moth at story slam competitions, and every one of those stories had to be five minutes or less. I sort of found my way through storytelling by telling five minute stories. And I think in the real world, a five minute story is a great length for a story. Have dinner with friends, you're driving in a car somewhere, you're playing golf with your buddies. Five minutes feels right. Doesn't mean that a one, two, or three minute story isn't excellent as well, but if given my choice, I'd be telling you five minute stories most of the time. But as a storyteller, I know that sometimes we don't have any time to tell stories. I know that sometimes we literally have seconds to make a connection with someone, to make an impression with someone, to make us known and remembered and perhaps even a little impactful on the people around us, even when we have just seconds to say something. This is what I call the subtle art of storytelling. It is the idea that when we tell stories as storytellers, the best versions of those stories tend to be one, two, three, five minute stories, sometimes 20 minute stories. But there are many moments, probably more moments than we know where we can be sharing of ourselves, attempting to connect to other people, and attempting to become memorable to other people through this subtle art of storytelling by saying things in infinitesimally small chunks of time. And as a storyteller, I seek to do that as well. I think all storytellers essentially seek to make themselves known and remembered and hopefully trusted and sort of impactful in a meaningful way, even if you have five seconds, even if you have an elevator ride, even if you have 30 seconds on an airplane before the person sitting next to you puts their headphones on and starts watching a movie. It's those moments that the true storytellers rise and make an impression. And so the subtle art of storytelling is what we're talking about today. It is the idea that when you meet people for the first time or for the second time or for the 100th time, and you want to let them know who you are, what you're about, what you believe in, what things about you might they connect with. As a storyteller, that is an area of opportunity that is often wasted by most people. But storytellers do not waste that opportunity. And I'm going to teach you how to not waste those opportunities today. It's a common thing that I end up talking with lots of people about. I often work with executives, vice presidents, CFOs, CEOs, who find themselves going off to conferences. And during their regular workday, they tend to be at the top of the food chain, the highest

## [03:03 - 06:07]

rung on the ladder. And so everyone they interact with is someone sort of below them in the food chain or below them on the rungs of the ladder. But when they go to a conference, oftentimes they're meeting with fellow CEOs, CFOs, vice presidents, C-suite people. And it's those times that a lot of these executives struggle. They recognize that they're going to be in some meeting, some outer conference area, some hallway in a convention center. They're going to meet up with someone and they're going to have 30 seconds, maybe a minute before they're sort of ushered off to some other place. And in that 30 seconds, that one minute, maybe 10 seconds, they want to know what to say. They want to know what to say so that they can leave a lasting impression on the person they just spoke to. This is where I teach the subtle art of storytelling. This is the process that I'm going to teach you to help you convey yourself to other people in a meaningful way. This comes up a lot when I'm dealing with executives who are going off to conferences in similar circumstances. Very often I'm working with a CEO or a vice president, a CFO, someone like that, for whom when they are at work, everyone is sort of always below their level. There's rarely someone operating at their level. Every company has one vice president of marketing, one CEO, one CFO. And so at work, they tend not to be around people who are operating at their level. And so there's a certain dynamic that is created. But these same people then go off to a conference, they go off to some experience where they're going to meet other CEOs or other vice presidents of marketing, people at their level. And a lot of times, oddly, or maybe not so oddly, they have a hard time figuring out what to say. They have a hard time figuring out what to share and how to make a connection, how to open a door, how to possibly create a meaningful relationship with another person. And this is what I teach them. I teach them how to do this subtle art of storytelling, meaning when you arrive, you're not going to sort of gather everyone around and spin a beautiful yarn. You're going to engage in a conversation with people. You're going to engage in a conversation. And hopefully by the end of that conversation, they want to speak to you more. They want to do business with you. They want to foster a friendship with you, whatever. The subtle art of storytelling is going to allow you to do this. So I'm going to teach you some strategies, the same strategies that I teach executives so that wherever you are, whether it's a little league baseball game or a funeral, whether it is a conference that you're attending for work or you are literally standing in line at Starbucks, you'll have the opportunity to use subtle storytelling to connect to the people around you, build your network, find new friends, find new business partners, find new customers, bring people closer to who you are. So let's begin strategy number one, listening. I know that everyone in the world thinks they're a great listener.

## [06:07 - 09:11]

I have never met anyone in my business who has told me they are a bad listener. Everyone believes that their ears work and because their ears work, they must be an excellent listener. It is not true. Most people I think are not very good listeners. Or at least let's put it this way. I don't think many people are strategic listeners. I don't think people enter into conversations or groups or opportunities to meet people with a strategic intention to listen in a way that will be productive to them later on. That is what I want from you. So here's a rule I follow every time I enter any kind of a situation that is social, whether it is I'm playing golf with three new people I've never met before. I'm attending a party. I'm going to a backyard cookout. I'm going to a baseball game. And I'm going to find myself sitting next to two new people wherever I am. What I do, the rule I follow is simply this. I try to be the last person to speak in every room or every space that I occupy. I try to aggressively be the last person to speak no matter what. At the same time, I shut off from my brain all the things that I want to say in response to the things people are saying. Someone sits down and says, I just got bit by a dog. My instinct is to talk about the time David Dunney's Doberman Pinscher bit me in the leg and they couldn't get the dog off my leg. But I push all that aside and leave it out of my brain because I am not a person who's looking to tell a story yet. I'm looking to be a listener. A listener in a strategic sense is a person who is gathering as much information as possible so that when you eventually do speak, you can deploy the best, most effective, most connective story possible. I try to be the last person to speak in every room because when I finally open my mouth, the things that come out of my mouth, the stories, the anecdotes, the examples, the metaphors, the jokes, all of those things are best tailored for the audience I have. Whether that audience is one person or a thousand people. I always try to let everyone else have an opportunity to speak before me, both because it strikes me as polite, but more importantly, because it's strategic. So developing a listening skill is going to help you with your subtle art of storytelling because one of the most effective things a storyteller can do is deploy the right story, the right anecdote, the right example, the right metaphor, the right joke at the right moment to have the greatest effect. In other words, you don't have to tell a five minute story. You don't have to spin a 10 minute yarn if you can say something that takes 20 seconds to say and yet lands directly in the hearts and minds of your audience because you are

## [09:11 - 12:17]

listening to them in a deep and profound way. So let me give you an example. A few months ago, I'm at Case Western University. I'm speaking to a bunch of scientists and medical professionals. And there's a moment when my workshop has been paused and we're going off to lunch and I find myself in the company of four people. I don't know these four people in any way other than faces and an audience that I was just speaking to for a couple hours. I don't know them at all. And the only way they know me is through the stories I've told on stages and the instruction I've been delivering. So I find myself in the company of these four people. And even though I'm the storyteller and I'm the presenter and I'm the one leading everything, I don't say a word. I stay quiet as long as possible. The first two people speak and then a question is turned to me. And because the other two people haven't had an opportunity to speak yet and we've only been talking for a couple minutes, I quickly answer the question and then flip it back to someone who hasn't spoken yet. And by the time that person's done, the fourth person, the one who has yet to speak, is now speaking. And now all four people have spoken in this group. I have been relatively silent for about five minutes at this point. I'm still perfectly content to remain silent. However, now I think I have something meaningful to say. The conversation amongst these four scientists centers around the difficulty that they are having, communicating the importance of their work to the people who are in charge of their institutions. Essentially they feel like people who are not being heard. So as I listen, I am trying to identify a theme, meaning, or message from the general tenor of the conversation. My goal is not to match content for content. My goal is not to listen to a story about not being heard and then immediately tell a story about not being heard. That is a content for content match. And it's fine, but it's only fine. When we content match, what we do is we fail to express empathy. Empathy comes not from listening to something someone has said, like I was once bit by a dog, and then tell your story about you being bit by a dog. That is not empathy. That is echoing. That is mirroring. In some cases, it's one-upping. And often, if you're a storyteller, feels like one-upping to the other person because your story is always better told and tends to be more interesting. So I am not in the business of content matching. I'm in the business of empathy because the subtle art of storytelling says when we express empathy to other human beings by being strategic listeners and figuring out what to say, people are going to feel more connected to us. So as I'm listening to these four scientists talk about how they are not being heard or not being listened to by the people who are in charge in their companies or their organizations, what I am asking myself is what are they really saying? What are they really trying to say? And I think, or at least I determine in that moment, that they are trying to say, we feel

## [12:17 - 15:17]

undervalued as human beings. We feel that the work we do is not being recognized by the people who matter most to us. We don't feel important enough in the organizations in which we are operating. And so knowing that, identifying that theme, meaning, or message, not the content that they're speaking, but the underlying sentiments that they are expressing, I find something I think is appropriate to say. I tell them this. I tell them that when I was about 10 years old, I was playing the flute in elementary school and I wasn't the best floutist, mostly because I wasn't practicing. And I was mostly not practicing because I didn't really have any parents telling me at home to practice. And as a 10-year-old boy, it turns out, without a parent sort of pushing you, you have to be a very special 10-year-old boy to practice and I was not a special 10-year-old boy. And I started to feel pretty lousy. Kim Solari and Joyce Alair were flute players in my group and they were fantastic. They were really good flute players and I was getting jealous of them. I was getting envious of what they were able to do. And so I decided, not because I wanted to be better, but because I didn't want to feel stupid anymore, I decided to start practicing. And I decided the thing I was going to do was I was going to memorize the next song. Not only was I going to learn how to play the next song, but I was going to memorize it so I could play it without music. And that's what I did. I spent hour after hour after hour sitting on my living room floor, playing my flute in front of my plastic plug-in fireplace, playing again and again and again and eventually learning how to play this song without reading the music. I was so proud of myself. I went to school the next day and I went to my flute lesson, which was weirdly in the boiler room of the elementary school where I attended. So I got into the boiler room and I sat down in this sort of dank room in a metal chair with the music stand in front of me. And before our teacher, Mr. Arnold came in to listen to us, I played the song for Kim and Joyce. And they were astounded. I think they were astounded because I had really never played very well and suddenly I could play a song from memory accurately. I think they were mostly astounded because of the change, the shift from one version of me to the other. But I felt good for the first time about playing the flute. And then Mr. Arnold came in and Kim, to my ever-loving gratitude, said, Mr. Arnold, you're not going to believe what Matt can do. Watch this. She set me up. And so I sat down in the chair and I faced Mr. Arnold and I played the song with the music stand

## [15:17 - 18:21]

turned the other way so that he knew I had memorized the song. And when I finished, I was so proud of myself. And Mr. Arnold looked at me and he said, well, that's great, but that's one song. You have a long way to go. I'll never forget that moment. I'll never forget the moment of feeling undervalued by a teacher, feeling less than worthy after one of my fellow students, a student who wasn't my friend in any way, but one of my fellow students recognized something I had done that was, in her mind, fairly extraordinary. And I hadn't done it for her. I had done it for him. And he had discounted all of my efforts and all of my enthusiasm and cost it into the garbage bin because it was my first attempt to be good at something. I live with that to this day. That's the story I told those scientists. Then I said to them, I understand what it's like to be undervalued. I understand how painful that is. I can imagine you must be having a really hard time. I matched theme, meaning and message and I made a really big impact on those scientists. I know I did. One of the scientists, one of the men in the group actually had a tear in his eye when I told him about Mr. Arnold. The reason that I connected so well with him is because I didn't tell a story about being at work and not being listened to. That would be a content match. Instead, I dug into my memory banks and said, when was a time in my life when I felt undervalued like they do? And instantly I went, oh, Mr. Arnold and the flute. I connected to that moment using theme, meaning and message. None of it happens unless I'm listening. None of it happens unless I let everyone in the group speak. None of it happens if when I'm asked a question, I then take over the conversation. But instead I'm asked a question, I answer it quickly and then turn it back on the people who haven't spoken yet. And none of it happens if I'm not strategically listening, meaning I'm listening to what they're saying. I'm clocking everything that they're reporting, but I'm also at the same time listening to the undercurrents, trying to identify what people are really saying under the surface. That is what a good listener does. Now, I told you that everyone thinks they're a great listener. Everyone I meet says, oh, I'm so good at listening. Let me ask you, when was the last time you did that? When was the last time you listened to someone speak? And while they were speaking, you were trying to figure out what they were really trying to say. What they were saying beneath the surface. When was the last time you met someone for the first time and in the midst of their conversation, you were asking yourself, what is this person really trying to convey? What are they really expressing? What is the feeling, the heart, the emotion beneath the story they're telling?

## [18:21 - 21:25]

That's strategic listening. And that will allow you to tell a story that is 20 seconds, 40 seconds, two minutes long, rather than some five or 10 minute epic. And you can land right in the hearts and minds of those people. That Mr. Arnold story probably took me about two minutes to tell those people. It was probably the shortest of all the stories told in that group. And yet I suspect it was the most impactful story told because I was listening to them and reaching out to them, not on a content to content match, but on an empathetic, deeply emotional, connective match listening, strategic listening, which will not happen today for you if you are not doing it. Meaning it's going to take some practice. You're not going to instantly become good at it. You have to start practicing and practicing and practicing it. Do it with your spouse, your kids, your colleagues, your neighbors, your friends. Whenever they're speaking to you, it is quite often that the story they're telling has an underlying message to it. They're trying to tell you something about their heart and their mind through what has happened to them in the outside world. You're seeking that. That is strategic listening. And that will allow you to subtly story tell, meaning you can tell short stories, anecdotes. You can offer examples and metaphors. You can make jokes that actually match the theme, meaning and message that people are expressing to you. That's the first step in the subtle art of storytelling. Strategy number two, dripping in personal data. So one of the best ways that a storyteller can engage in the subtle art of storytelling is by informing people about deep and important things about you in a casual, off-handed, but strategic way. Meaning they're not going to know you're doing it and yet you're going to be doing it. There are a few things I want you to know about me all the time. I want you to know that I'm a parent. I want you to know that I have a son named Charlie and a daughter named Clara. I want you to know that my daughter is a teenager and my son is 11 years old. I want you to know all that because when you hear that I'm a parent and that I have two kids, if you are a parent and you have children, you're going to feel more connected to me for sure. Also, even if you don't have kids, you were once a kid and you once had a dad. Maybe you still do. Again, you're going to feel more connected to me because of that. I always want you to know that I'm married and that my wife's name is Alicia. I want you to know I'm married because that means that there is a human being in this world who has theoretically agreed to spend her entire life in my company. That means someone is willing to spend their entire life with me, which is sort of like verification that I am an okay person. That for all of my flaws and foibles, some human being loves me enough to actually live with me,

## [21:26 - 24:28]

sleep in the same bed with me, eat most meals with me, and spend the majority of their time with me. That's a fairly astounding thing. If you're not married, I'm not condemning you. I'm not saying that you're at any fault for not being married. Congratulations, you're an independent person. If you recently divorced, congratulations. You probably found freedom that you desperately needed. For whatever reason, if you're single, that's fine. But if you're married, you should want people to know you're married because it really does mean that there's some proof positive that you at least have hygiene and you're probably employed and you're mildly entertaining and you eat with silverware. All of these things come with saying, I have a spouse and that spouse loves me. And by naming that spouse, Alicia, that person becomes more tangible to you because that's what happens when we use names. One of the subtle arts of storytelling is make sure you use names at all times. As soon as we name a person, they become more tangible and real. They're also more easy to imagine. If you don't know what my wife looks like and I say her name is Alicia, you have a platonic image of all Alicia's in your head. You actually imagine Alicia for me based upon some Alicia you used to know, some Alicia you currently know, or some version of Alicia that your brain has assembled a long time ago. By giving you names, these people become more real to you. So I want you to know my parent. I want you to know I have a spouse. I want you to know I'm an elementary school teacher. Why? Everyone loves elementary school teachers. You never want to pay us enough, but you love us and you honor the work we do. And that's important to me. So I tell you, I'm an elementary school teacher and instantly you like me more. I want you to know that I have two cats, Toby and Pluto. I want you to know that because if you have cats, you understand how crazy I am about my cats. And if you don't have cats, that's okay. It doesn't sort of hurt me in any way. It actually tells you that I'm a pet owner and I care about things other than human beings. It's very effective. I want you to know that I'm a golfer. You are probably not a golfer because less than 10% of Americans play golf on any regular basis. But if you are a golfer, you feel deeply connected to me knowing I also play golf. Some of the things that make us who we are create intense connections with other people. Some less intense. What we're looking to do is identify the characteristics that make us us and then find ways to drip those things into our lives. And so I have created a worksheet to help you. with other human beings. So you can see across the top, we have a topic. We have my status in regards to that topic. So as we look at it, you can see my relationship status as I'm married for 16 years to Alicia. The total addressable market here is high because most people are in a relationship, not everyone. And if you're not, no offense, but most people are married or in a relationship. And the intensity of the connection tends to be pretty moderate, meaning if I have a spouse and you have a spouse, we feel connected. We have stuff to share with each other. Okay. Right? So college affiliation. Military history. I have none, but I happen to know that if I had been in the Navy and I meet someone who had been in the Navy, we're going to instantly feel connected. ## [30:32 - 33:38]

a lot of people care about it. It creates intense connections as a result. So this is my North Star. This is the way that I identify when I'm in a group of people what I should be talking about. This allows me to have a selection of things to choose from. You're going to have this same spreadsheet. You're going to fill the spreadsheet out doing the same thing, identifying the total addressable market. How many people really care about this thing or have done this thing or are involved in this thing right now? And then what kind of intensity does that produce in terms of a connection, right? Is this a running intensity where runners absolutely love runners no matter what, or is it like reading, which is sure everyone reads and we don't mind talking about books, but it's not going to be exactly the most intense connection we can have with people. This personal interest inventory will help you with the subtle art of storytelling, because it'll let you know what to drip into your life. It'll let you know how to answer questions. It'll let you know what people will care about and what people will not. Let me give you some examples. So dripping this information into your life. A friend asks me the other day, how are you doing? That is a question you get asked all the time. How are you? How are you doing? How's it going? You have two choices in that situation. You can answer just like everybody else and say, fine, good, not so good, okay. That is the answer that everyone answers. Don't be everyone. Be a storyteller. Instead, if you ask me ever, how am I doing? I am going to answer by dripping personal information into my answer. The other day, a friend in the hallway says, how are you doing? And I said, fantastic. Celtics won last night. Yankees won last night. My friend says, you're a Yankees fan? I didn't know that. And I said, yeah, I'm a Yankees fan. You like the Yankees? She said, no, I'm a Red Sox fan. Now we have something to talk about. Right? So rather than saying I'm good, rather than saying I'm fine, rather than saying I'm great, I say how I feel by indicating something about myself. I'm a storyteller at all times. I am constantly dripping that information into my life at all times. Whenever I can, I'm going to let someone know I'm a Yankees fan. I'm going to let them know I'm a Patriot season ticket holder. I'm going to let them know I have two kids at home who I love but can sometimes be a pain in the butt individually and collectively. I'm going to let them know I have a spouse who I love named Alicia. I'm going to let them know about my cats, Toby and Pluto. And when Toby escapes in the morning and hides under the car and someone asks me how my day was, I'm going to say, it didn't start out great because my cat Toby got out of the house again and instantly ran under the car where he hid and stayed hidden until we could finally get him out. If you ask me on this day how I'm doing, I might say, well, I began the day by speaking to a woman in Tokyo about storytelling. And as I spoke to her, two deer crossed my backyard into the neighbor's backyard and then ran away.

## [33:38 - 36:41]

My day started out pretty great. It's still going pretty well. That's what I would say. That is me not telling a story but dripping information wherever I can into my life. Here's one of the trickiest ways I do it. Oftentimes, I find myself going to a conference to deliver a workshop and I need to connect my laptop to their screen. And what I will do is I will set my computer up to kick over to a screensaver after 30 seconds of inactivity. And that screensaver is always pictures of my family. I will pretend as if I didn't know the screensaver was going to kick on and I will intentionally not look at the screen for a while so that pictures of my family and my cats and my friends and my fun will scroll across the screen. And then at some point, I'll look up at the screen and say, oh, I'm sorry, I didn't realize the screensaver was on and I'll go up and I'll turn it off. But it was intentional. I want these people before I say a word to know things about me. I want to drip my life into their world. And so I want a picture of my wife to go by with me, the two of us face to face smiling. I want a picture of my kids to go by. I want a picture of my cats to go by. I want a picture of my golfing buddies to go by. I want a picture of me and my friend Shep at the New England Patriots, AFC Championship game in 2019. I want all these pictures to scroll by so they know me before I begin speaking. That is the subtle art of storytelling by dripping yourself into the lives of other people by first identifying the things that you should be talking about and then finding opportunities to speak about them, to show them to people, to let people know who you are. Never answer a question without inserting a small bit of information whenever possible. But it begins by knowing what to say. So complete the worksheet, find all the things that make you who you are, identify the addressable markets and level of intensity for each one of those things, and then find ways to drip those things into your life wherever possible. This is not his traditional outfit when he comes to school every day. He doesn't dress unprofessionally, but he does not dress like this. He's an elementary school teacher like me. And although there are elementary school teachers who wear suits and ties every day, I don't understand any of them. Most elementary school teachers are dressing sort of business

## [36:41 - 39:44]

casual or in the case of me, casual casual. But this guy typically wears khakis and a polo shirt or a sweater, pair of shoes. He's presentable and looking like someone who has a job and is ready to do that job. But on this day, he is dressed to the nines. He is wearing his suit and his tie shiny shoes, and it strikes fear in my heart because I suddenly realized that I've forgotten something. It is the first day of parent teacher conferences, and I have forgotten. And so I need to race to my room to figure out which parent teacher conferences I have scheduled for this day so that I can be prepared. I knew it was parent teacher conference day because my colleague was dressed in a suit. And that, I believe, is disastrous. I believe that is terrible subtext. This teacher on any other given day wears business casual. I'm going to make you think that I'm super professional, even though you see me every other day in khakis and a polo, when you come in and meet with me for a parent teacher conference, for some reason, this is a highly formal affair. I believe the subtext here is terrible. I believe what the subtext does for this teacher is creates a barrier between him and the parents he's speaking to. I believe it sends a message to the parents that he is going to be less than authentic in the meetings they have. want these parents to see me as another human being who loves their kid, probably not as much as they do, but close. I want them to see us as a team. best, but the person, Trevor Devine, is who we purchased windows from. We chose Trevor Devine. Trevor Devine understood subtext in a brilliant way. That doesn't make you feel very important. The subtext there is we're a numbers game. I need to meet with three people tonight. If you need a little extra time, you're not going to get it. minutes and five minutes and sometimes 10 minutes or an hour. Sometimes people just sit and listen to us and it's a glorious thing, but most of the time we don't have that kind of time. Those are ways to tell stories too, and those are ways that we can tell stories repeatedly, constantly, and relentlessly throughout the day. Most people don't think about these things.
